tasks.title: Vowel Counter
tasks.description:
Strings are fundamental data structures in programming, representing text. Analyzing text often involves counting specific types of characters, such as vowels, which can be useful in linguistics, text processing, or even simple data validation tasks. Understanding how to iterate through a string and apply conditional logic to its characters is a core skill.

Your program must read a single line of text from standard input, which will be treated as a string. It should then count the total number of vowels (a, e, i, o, u) present in the string, treating both uppercase and lowercase letters as the same (e.g., 'A' should count as 'a'). The input string will have a maximum length of 100 characters, including the null terminator. Finally, print the total count of vowels to standard output.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: Hello World → count: 0 → 1 ('e') → 2 ('o') → 3 ('o'). Output: 3

tasks.title: Max Element and First Index
tasks.description:
Arrays are powerful for organizing collections of related data, such as measurement series, sensor readings, or financial records. A common analytical task is to identify the largest element within such a collection and know its position, which can be critical for further processing, data analysis, or decision-making.

Your program must first read an integer `N` from standard input (where `1 <= N <= 100`), representing the number of elements in an array. Subsequently, it will read `N` integers, one by one, into an array. Your program must then find the maximum integer in this array and output its value, followed by a single space, and then its *first* 0-based index.

WORKED EXAMPLE:
Input: 5\n10\n4\n20\n15\n20 → max_val: 10 → 20, max_idx: 0 → 2. Output: 20 2

tasks.title: Simple Caesar Cipher
tasks.description:
Strings are sequences of characters, and text manipulation is a core programming skill with applications ranging from data processing to cryptography. Encryption, even simple forms like the Caesar cipher, demonstrates how character arrays can be transformed systematically by applying a consistent rule to each character. The Caesar cipher shifts each letter in the plaintext by a certain number of places down or up the alphabet.

Your program must read a single line of text (a string) from standard input, followed by an integer `shift` value (where `0 <= shift <= 25`). The input string will have a maximum length of 100 characters. It should then encrypt the input string using a Caesar cipher. Only alphabetic characters (A-Z, a-z) should be shifted. Non-alphabetic characters (numbers, spaces, punctuation) must remain unchanged. The shift should wrap around the alphabet (e.g., 'Z' shifted by 1 becomes 'A', 'z' shifted by 1 becomes 'a'). Finally, print the encrypted string to standard output.
